# Brief: Meg (PERSONA-011)
## Upcoming Meeting: 2026-03-30

## Since Last Meeting

No new evidence has surfaced in the seven days since the March 23 workshop. No Sentry coverage, no board communications, no community letters appear in the record.

That silence matters for Meg specifically: she is walking into what was explicitly billed as the vote meeting — March 30 was named at the workshop as the decision date — without any updated sourcing beyond her own Notes app from last week. Her group chats have been quiet because nothing new has dropped. But her networks are watching. Anyone who attended or followed the March 23 workshop knows tonight is the night, and her accuracy-first reputation means the first post she sends after this meeting will carry extra weight. She has one week of unanswered questions and no new documents to cross-reference.

The absence of a public legal memo on the Title VI question is also notable. Seven days have passed since the Kayler closure recommendation was formally named, and no public analysis has appeared. If the district commissioned one in that window, it hasn't been released. That may be the most important thing that didn't happen this week.

---

## Open Questions

Carrying into March 30 from the cumulative record, in Meg's terms:

- **Did they actually get a Title VI legal opinion before tonight?** I have to know this before I post anything about the Kayler vote. If they're voting without one, that's the story — and that's something people in my chats will ask me about.
- **Is the Kayler vote actually happening tonight, or is it getting pushed?** The Title VI question, the Option A/B split, and the board dynamics all give individual members a reason to move to table. I need to be ready for a delay and know *why* it happened.
- **What exactly is Option A versus Option B, and which schools does each one affect?** I have kids at an elementary and a middle school. Some of my group chat parents have kids at schools that land differently depending on which option passes. I need to be able to say "if Option A passes, here's what it means for [school]."
- **When did the number grow from $7.2M to $8.4M, and why?** I've been posting $7.2M since December. Councilor Matthews said $8.4M on the record March 19. Nobody has reconciled that. If someone cites a number tonight, I need to know which one it is and whether it's been updated again.
- **Do the 72 pink slips actually map to the 78 positions?** That's a six-position gap I haven't been able to explain. If the scope of cuts changed, I posted the wrong number.
- **For the 32 teachers on the recall list — is that real protection, or is it mostly on paper?** Parents are going to ask me whether their kid's teacher is coming back. I need to understand whether the recall list has openings to absorb those 32 people.
- **Is anyone going to break down the cuts by building tonight?** That question has been open since December 17. My group chats are school-specific. I can't post a useful summary if it's still all-district aggregate.
- **Will DeAngelis apply the same equity standard to Kayler that she applied to Skillin?** She made the "west of the Dairy Queen" argument on Skillin's behalf on March 2. Kayler's population is approximately 45% BIPOC and 30–35% multilingual learners. If she votes yes on closing Kayler, I need to be able to explain that clearly and accurately — or flag the inconsistency.
- **With FY26 ending in deficit and no fund balance remaining, is FY27 actually the last year of this crisis, or are we looking at multi-year damage on the same property tax bill?**

---

## Agenda Implications

No agenda has been published for tonight's meeting. Based on the cumulative record, the following topics are highly likely to come up — and here is what each means for Meg's job:

**Kayler Elementary closure vote**
This is the centerpiece of the evening and the reason Meg's networks are watching. The motion will likely be explicit: vote to approve the superintendent's recommendation to close Kayler as part of the elementary reconfiguration package. What this means for Meg: she needs the exact vote count, the exact names of any no votes, and any stated reasons. If someone raises the Title VI question from the floor, she needs to capture that verbatim — it is the kind of procedural move that will prompt immediate questions in her group chats ("wait, can they even do this legally?"). She also needs to listen for whether the vote passes with conditions attached, such as a required legal review before implementation, which would be a different relay than a clean yes.

**Option A/B decision**
This remains open from March 23 and is the more complicated relay challenge. Option A and Option B both involve elementary grade-band transitions, but they differ in how many schools transition and when. For Meg, the key information is: which families are affected by which option, and what is the specific impact on each building? She should prepare to ask directly (or listen for) the school-by-school breakdown rather than waiting to decode it from aggregate language. This is exactly the kind of information that does not survive the translation from a three-hour meeting to five iMessage bullets — she needs to hear it named specifically.

**Budget parameters / cut package update**
The March 23 workshop built the budget presentation within the 6% ceiling; March 30 may be the first formal board action on the budget resolution itself, or it may be structured as a vote on the cut package that supports the budget. Meg should watch for:
- Whether the $8.4M figure is used, and whether anyone explains the gap from $7.2M
- Whether any cuts are broken out by school building for the first time
- Whether the early retirement buyout option ($1M+ potential savings, 41 eligible staff) is acted on or quietly dropped
- Any mention of the FY26 deficit and what city fund balance repayment looks like

**Possible procedural moves that affect the meeting itself**
Given the Title VI question and the compressed timeline (April 7 public hearing is one week out), there is a real possibility of a motion to table, a motion to delay pending legal review, or a motion to split the votes. Meg should note any procedural move precisely — who made it, who seconded it, and the final count — because procedural outcomes are exactly the kind of thing that gets misreported in the hours after a meeting.

---

## Watch For

- **Whether a Title VI legal memo is distributed before the vote begins.** If it was commissioned and exists, it may appear in the board packet tonight. If no written analysis is presented and the board votes anyway, that is a factual relay point — "the board voted to close Kayler without a formal civil rights legal review" — and Meg should be ready to post it accurately, not editorialize it.
- **The exact motion language on the Kayler vote.** "Approve the closure of Kayler Elementary" is a different relay than "approve the reconfiguration plan including the recommended closure of Kayler Elementary pending legal review." The difference matters for her networks.
- **DeAngelis's vote and any statement she makes.** She is the person whose position is most in tension — equity champion who blocked Skillin on demographic grounds, now facing a closure recommendation with a majority-BIPOC and multilingual learner student body. If she votes yes, any reasoning she puts on the record will be the thing her networks question first.
- **Any board member who cites the Title VI question as grounds for a no vote or a motion to table.** Capture the name and the argument verbatim if possible.
- **The specific deficit figure used at the podium tonight.** If anyone says $8.4M, $7.2M, or a new number, note it and note who said it. The discrepancy between those two figures is the one active supersession Meg has not been able to resolve, and it affects every relay she sends.
- **Any school-by-school cut breakdown.** If the administration presents position cuts by building for the first time tonight — even in a slide — screenshot it immediately. This is the information her group chats need that has been withheld as an aggregate figure since December 17.
- **The vote count on every motion, not just the outcome.** A 5-2 vote to close Kayler tells a different story than a 4-3 vote, and the names of the no votes tell the story her networks will want to know by morning.
- **Auto-caption failures on school names and dollar figures.** "Kayler" and "Dyer" and specific dollar figures are exactly the proper nouns the SPC-TV auto-captions misread most. When a slide is on screen with a number or school name, screenshot it — don't trust the caption.
- **Any mention of the recall list reality for the 32 affected teachers.** If administration or a board member addresses how many openings realistically exist versus how many names are on the list, that is a significant update to the teacher cut relay she has been using since March 23.
- **Whether anyone explicitly links the April 7 public hearing to the Kayler vote tonight.** If the board frames tonight's vote as provisional pending community feedback at April 7, that is a materially different outcome than a final vote — and it changes what Meg tells her networks about whether there is still a chance to change it.
- **Tipton's and Matthews's positions.** Both have made on-record statements in the past month that established specific postures on equity (Tipton's yes on Project HOME) and on fiscal numbers ($8.4M from Matthews). Watch for either of them to anchor or move from those positions.
